population: Patients admitted to ICU with sepsis in a Brazilian public university ICU
notes: Retrospective cohort study 2010-2015; 19-bed public university ICU in Brazil

population: Patients admitted to ICU with sepsis in a Brazilian public university ICU
notes: Compared against Sepsis-2 definitions for mortality stratification accuracy

population: Patients admitted to ICU with sepsis in a Brazilian public university ICU
method: Net Reclassification Index (NRI) and Integrated Discrimination Index (IDI)
notes: Evaluated whether addition of lactate to each stratum of Sepsis-2 and Sepsis-3 definitions improved mortality stratification

population: ICU patients with infection/sepsis in a Brazilian ICU
predictor: Sepsis-3 category: infection with no organ dysfunction
method: Comparison of mortality rates across categories
notes: Mortality progressively higher across Sepsis-3 categories: no organ dysfunction 7%, sepsis 25%, septic shock 46%

population: ICU patients with infection/sepsis in a Brazilian ICU
method: Comparison of mortality rates across categories
notes: Mortality progressively higher across Sepsis-3 categories; Sepsis-3 definitions superior to Sepsis-2 in stratifying mortality

population: ICU patients with infection/sepsis in a Brazilian ICU
method: Comparison of mortality rates across categories
notes: Mortality progressively higher across Sepsis-3 categories; Sepsis-3 definitions superior to Sepsis-2 in stratifying mortality

population: ICU patients with infection/sepsis in a Brazilian ICU
method: Comparison of mortality rates across categories

population: ICU patients with infection/sepsis in a Brazilian ICU
method: Comparison of mortality rates across categories
performance: P<0.001 across severe sepsis vs. septic shock
notes: Sepsis-2 mortality only significantly different across severe sepsis and septic shock categories

population: ICU patients with infection/sepsis in a Brazilian ICU
method: Comparison of mortality rates across categories
performance: P<0.001 across severe sepsis vs. septic shock
notes: Sepsis-2 mortality only significantly different across severe sepsis and septic shock categories

population: ICU patients with infection/sepsis in a Brazilian ICU
method: Multivariate analysis and combined lactate-definition accuracy assessment
performance: Improved accuracy in severe sepsis and septic shock groups (Sepsis-2); no-dysfunction and septic shock groups (Sepsis-3)
notes: Lactate levels below 4 mmol/L did not improve definitions' accuracy in stratifying ICU mortality; multivariate analysis confirmed similar findings

timing: First day of ICU admission (with second day or median imputation for <5% missing values)
method: AUROC comparison using DeLong's test, continuous NRI, and IDI
notes: Reclassification cutoffs used: <30%, 30–60%, and >60% probability of ICU mortality. Categorical NRI not reported due to no single probability cutoff defined for decision-making in septic patients per Sepsis-3 consensus. 46 (<5%) missing lactate values imputed.
Mean age for whole group was 52 years

method: Pearson's Chi-squared test with post hoc analyses
effect_size: Sepsis: 7% (7/135 deaths), Severe sepsis: 17% (43/252 deaths), Septic shock: 46% (198/572 deaths)
performance: P < 0.001 (overall); Sepsis vs Severe sepsis P < 0.001; Severe sepsis vs Septic shock P < 0.001
notes: Mortality stratified according to Sepsis-2 definition

method: Pearson's Chi-squared test with post hoc analyses
effect_size: No-dysfunction: 13% (18/103 deaths), Sepsis: 25% (106/419 deaths), Septic shock: 44% (250/435 deaths)
performance: P < 0.001 (overall); No-dysfunction vs Sepsis P = 0.430; Sepsis vs Septic shock P < 0.001; No-dysfunction vs Septic shock P < 0.001
notes: Mortality stratified according to Sepsis-3 definition

effect_size: Whole group: 5 days, Survivors: 5 days, Non-survivors: 6 days

predictor: Discharge within the first 48 h of ICU

population: ICU patients with sepsis classified by Sepsis-2 and Sepsis-3 definitions
method: Multivariate logistic regression with and without lactate inclusion
performance: Clear stratification of mortality among three categories (no-dysfunction, sepsis, septic shock)
notes: Sepsis-3 classification reduced the percentage of septic shock patients compared to Sepsis-2 definition

population: ICU patients with sepsis classified by Sepsis-2 and Sepsis-3 definitions
method: Descriptive mortality rate analysis by lactate category
effect_size: Lactate >4 mmol/L associated with clearly increased mortality
performance: Clearly marked increased mortality in severe sepsis and septic shock (Sepsis-2) and in no-dysfunction and septic shock categories (Sepsis-3)
notes: Lactate >2 mmol/L was not clearly discriminative of mortality in Sepsis-2 defined categories of severe sepsis or septic shock

population: ICU patients with sepsis classified by Sepsis-2 and Sepsis-3 definitions
method: Multivariate logistic regression adjusted for lactate level, age, and illness severity
notes: Table 3 reports association of Sepsis-3 definition with ICU mortality after adjustment for lactate, age, and illness severity; full results not provided in excerpt

population: Septic patients admitted to a strained ICU in a developing country
performance: Progressive mortality strata across all three Sepsis-3 categories
notes: Sepsis-3 clearly categorized patients along a spectrum of severity with statistically significant differences across all categories

population: Septic patients admitted to a strained ICU in a developing country
performance: Only septic shock criterion was associated with significantly higher mortality compared to severe sepsis and sepsis categories
notes: Sepsis-2 lacked accuracy in stratifying mortality risk across sepsis and severe sepsis categories

population: Septic patients admitted to a strained ICU in a developing country
method: Comparison of mortality prediction accuracy with and without lactate criterion (Table 3)
performance: Improved mortality prediction accuracy on both Sepsis-2 and Sepsis-3 definitions
notes: High lactate may reflect perfusion abnormalities or stress hyperlactatemia; results referenced in Table 3

population: Patients without organ dysfunction (SOFA score <2) with high lactate levels
performance: Categorizes a population at higher risk of death
notes: In patients without organ dysfunction as assessed by SOFA score, lactate >4 mmol/L identified a higher-risk population. Lactate >2 mmol/L did not add prognostic information in septic shock stratum (already included in definition). Lactate was a prognostic factor for both Sepsis-3 definitions and reclassified some Sepsis-3-defined patients when levels were above 4 mmol/L.

population: Infected patients admitted to a strained ICU in a middle-income country
predictor: Sepsis-3 clinical criteria (new definition of sepsis)
performance: Progressive prognostic value across all three categories (no-dysfunction, sepsis, septic shock)
notes: Sepsis-2 definitions did not show progressive prognostic value in the same sample. Serum lactate >4 mmol/L improved accuracy in the no-dysfunction and septic shock groups.

population: Infected patients admitted to a strained ICU in a middle-income country
performance: Improved accuracy for values >4 mmol/L in the no-dysfunction and septic shock groups
notes: Lactate improvement in accuracy was specifically noted for the no-dysfunction and septic shock categories under Sepsis-3 classification.
